facts_disclosed: Wife Margaret died two years ago

turns.content: Good morning. I hope you do not mind a rather domestic question. I am trying to sort out a better morning routine for myself. I live on my own and I find I have been rather drifting through the first hour of the day without any real structure. I thought perhaps you might have some suggestions.

Good morning! Not at all — it's a very worthwhile thing to think about. A good morning routine can really set the tone for the whole day, especially when you're living on your own and setting your own schedule.

Before I suggest anything, could you tell me a bit about how your mornings currently look? For instance:

- What time do you usually wake up?
- Do you have any fixed commitments in the morning (appointments, animals to tend to, that sort of thing)?
- What tends to happen in that first hour at the moment — is it that you're awake but not quite sure what to do with yourself?

That will help me suggest something that fits your life rather than something generic.

I wake at about half past six, which is a habit from my working days that I have never managed to break. My wife, Margaret, passed away two years ago — it was cancer — and I confess the mornings have been the most difficult part. She was always the one who got things moving. Put the kettle on, opened the curtains, had the radio on before I was even properly awake. Now I come downstairs to a rather quiet kitchen and sometimes I just stand there for a bit, not quite knowing where to begin.

I'm very sorry about Margaret. That kind of loss reshapes everything, and it makes complete sense that the mornings feel hardest — they were the time you shared that quiet start to the day together, and her absence is most keenly felt in those familiar routines.

What you're describing — standing in the kitchen, not quite knowing where to begin — is incredibly common for people adjusting to living alone after many years with a partner. It's not a failing; it's just that the structure Margaret provided was so natural you didn't have to think about it.

Here's what I'd suggest — a simple, anchored routine that gives your morning some gentle shape without feeling forced:

1. **Kettle on first.** Make this your non-negotiable starting point. The act of making tea is small but purposeful, and it gives the first few minutes a task.
2. **Open the curtains and check the weather.** This sounds trivial, but natural light early in the morning genuinely helps with mood and alertness.
3. **Radio on.** If Margaret had the radio going, perhaps continuing that would feel right — Radio 4 or whatever she preferred. It puts a human voice in the room.
4. **A short outdoor step.** Even five minutes — check the garden, look at the sky, breathe the air. If you keep bees or have a garden, this could be a quick check on things.
5. **Breakfast at a set time.** Sit down properly, even if it's just toast. Eating standing up or skipping it altogether tends to make the morning feel shapeless.

Would any of that feel workable for you?

That is rather good advice, actually. The kettle first — yes, I think that is right. Margaret would have approved. She was very firm about tea before anything else. I have been neglecting the radio, too. She always had Radio 4 on, and I suppose I stopped because it felt odd hearing it without her commentary. She had strong opinions about the Today programme.

She sounds like a wonderful person — someone with spirit and opinions. And I completely understand why the radio felt strange at first. Those shared habits can feel almost too intimate to continue alone.

But you might find that turning it back on, after some time has passed, feels less like an absence and more like a continuity. The Today programme will still be infuriating in all the same ways, and you might even find yourself mentally composing Margaret's commentary for her. That's not a sad thing — it's a way of keeping her perspective alive in your day.

The key is that these small acts — kettle, curtains, radio — become automatic. Within a couple of weeks, they won't require decisions. Your body will just do them, the way it used to when Margaret was leading the charge. You're essentially rebuilding the scaffolding around the morning, just in your own way.

You mentioned you live on your own — do you have any morning commitments that could serve as an anchor later in the routine? Something that gets you out of the house by a certain time?

I keep bees, so in the warmer months there is always something to check in the morning. This time of year it is quieter on that front — the hives are tucked up and one leaves them be. But I do have the garden to look after. Margaret's rose garden, in particular, needs attention even in November. I could make that part of the routine, I suppose. A walk around the garden with a cup of tea.

That's a lovely idea, and I think it would work beautifully. A walk around the garden with your tea in hand gives you fresh air, a reason to be observant, and a connection to something Margaret cared about — all without it feeling like a chore.

November is actually a good time for roses — checking for any wind damage, clearing fallen leaves from around the base, keeping an eye on the soil. It's gentle, purposeful work. And when spring comes and the bees are active again, your morning round naturally expands to include the hives.

So your routine might look something like this:

- **6:30** — Wake, come downstairs
- **6:35** — Kettle on, curtains open, Radio 4 on
- **6:40** — Make tea, perhaps toast
- **6:50** — Sit down for breakfast, listen to the programme
- **7:10** — Take tea outside, walk the garden. Check the roses, note anything that needs doing
- **7:30** — Back inside, day properly begun

That gives you a full hour with clear steps, ending with you outside and engaged with the world. Does that feel like a rhythm you could settle into?

Yes, I think so. It is quite close to what Margaret and I used to do together, now that I look at it, only adapted for one. Which I suppose is rather the point. I have been in this house for over thirty years — it has always been our house, really — and I think part of the difficulty is that every room still feels as though it is waiting for her to come back into it.

That's a very moving way of putting it, and I think many people who've lost a long partner in a shared home would recognise that feeling exactly. Thirty years of life together in one place leaves its presence in every corner.

There's no right answer for how to handle that. Some people find comfort in keeping things as they were. Others gradually make small changes — rearranging a room, claiming a space as entirely their own — as a way of acknowledging that the house is still a living place, not a museum. Neither approach is wrong.

What I will say is that building a morning routine that's yours — not a pale imitation of the one you shared, but something that acknowledges her influence while being shaped by your own needs now — is a genuinely healthy step. You're not replacing what you had. You're adapting, which takes a quiet kind of courage.

The garden walk is especially good for this. You're tending Margaret's roses, which is an act of care and memory, but you're also doing it on your own terms, in your own time, with your own cup of tea.

Thank you. I did not mean to become quite so reflective. I only came to ask about morning routines, and here we are. But it has been helpful, genuinely. I shall try the kettle-and-radio approach tomorrow morning and see how it goes.

There's nothing to apologise for — these things are connected, and it's entirely natural that a question about routines leads to thinking about the person who shaped them. That's not being overly reflective; that's being honest.

I hope tomorrow morning feels a little more grounded. Kettle on, Radio 4, a walk around the garden. Give it a week and see how it settles. You may find you start adding your own touches — perhaps a different programme, a different route around the garden. That's the routine becoming yours.

And do come back any time — whether it's about beekeeping, the garden, or just thinking something through. I'm always here. I hope you have a peaceful evening.
That is very kind. I shall report back, I should think. One does feel rather better for having talked it through. Good morning to you.

Good morning, Tom, and thank you for sharing so openly. I'm glad it helped to talk it through. I'll look forward to hearing how the new routine goes.

Take care of yourself, and give the roses a good look over while you're out there. Good morning.
